(Māori pronunciation: [[ˈtaʉɾaŋa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/M%C4%81ori "Help:IPA/Māori") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Tauranga_Pronunciation.wav "File:Tauranga Pronunciation.wav"), Māori language for "resting place," or "safe anchorage")[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) is a coastal city in the [Bay of Plenty Region](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty_Region "Bay of Plenty Region") and the [fifth-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_New_Zealand "List of cities in New Zealand") of [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), with an urban population of 160,900 (June 2025),[[5]](#cite_note-NZ_population_data_2018-5) or roughly 3% of the national population. It was settled by Māori late in the 13th century and colonised by Europeans in the early 19th century. It was constituted as a city in 1963.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

**Tauranga** (Māori pronunciation: [[ˈtaʉɾaŋa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/M%C4%81ori "Help:IPA/Māori") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Tauranga_Pronunciation.wav "File:Tauranga Pronunciation.wav"), Māori language for "resting place," or "safe anchorage")[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) is a coastal city in the [Bay of Plenty Region](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty_Region "Bay of Plenty Region") and the [fifth-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_New_Zealand "List of cities in New Zealand") of [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), with an urban population of 160,900 (June 2025),[[5]](#cite_note-NZ_population_data_2018-5) or roughly 3% of the national population. It was settled by Māori late in the 13th century and colonised by Europeans in the early 19th century. It was constituted as a city in 1963.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The city lies in the northwestern corner of the Bay of Plenty, on the southeastern edge of [Tauranga Harbour](/wiki/Tauranga_Harbour "Tauranga Harbour"). The city extends over an area of 141.91 square kilometres (54.79 sq mi), and encompasses the communities of [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem,_New_Zealand "Bethlehem, New Zealand"), on the southwestern outskirts of the city; [Greerton](/wiki/Greerton "Greerton"), on the southern outskirts of the city; Matua, west of the central city overlooking Tauranga Harbour; Maungatapu; [Mount Maunganui](/wiki/Mount_Maunganui "Mount Maunganui"), located north of the central city across the harbour facing the Bay of Plenty; [Otūmoetai](/wiki/Ot%C5%ABmoetai "Otūmoetai"); [Papamoa](/wiki/Papamoa "Papamoa"), Tauranga's largest suburb, located in the Bay of Plenty; Tauranga City; [Tauranga South](/wiki/Tauranga_South "Tauranga South"); and [Welcome Bay](/wiki/Welcome_Bay "Welcome Bay").

Tauranga is one of New Zealand's main centres for business, international trade, culture, fashion and horticultural science. The [Port of Tauranga](/wiki/Port_of_Tauranga "Port of Tauranga") is New Zealand's largest port in terms of gross export tonnage and efficiency.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Tauranga is one of New Zealand's fastest-growing cities, with an 11% increase in population between the 2006 census and the 2013 census,[[11]](#cite_note-11) and 19% between the 2013 and 2018 census. Due to its rapid population growth, Tauranga has become New Zealand's fifth-largest city, overtaking [Dunedin](/wiki/Dunedin "Dunedin") and the [Napier](/wiki/Napier,_New_Zealand "Napier, New Zealand")-[Hastings](/wiki/Hastings,_New_Zealand "Hastings, New Zealand") urban areas.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [List of historic places in Tauranga](/wiki/List_of_historic_places_in_Tauranga "List of historic places in Tauranga")

### Settlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Settlement")]

The earliest known settlers were [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people"), who arrived in the 13th century at Tauranga in the [Tākitimu](/wiki/T%C4%81kitimu "Tākitimu") and the [Mātaatua](/wiki/M%C4%81taatua "Mātaatua") [waka](/wiki/Waka_(canoe) "Waka (canoe)").

At 9 am on Friday, 23 June 1826, [*Herald*](/wiki/Herald_(1826_ship) "Herald (1826 ship)") was the first European ship to enter [Tauranga Harbour](/wiki/Tauranga_Harbour "Tauranga Harbour"). The Revd [Henry Williams](/wiki/Henry_Williams_(missionary) "Henry Williams (missionary)") conducted a Christian service at Otamataha Pā.[[12]](#cite_note-HW2-1-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald2011-4-13)[[14]](#cite_note-McCauley-14)

In December 1826 and again in March 1827, the *Herald* travelled to Tauranga from the [Bay of Islands](/wiki/Bay_of_Islands "Bay of Islands") to obtain supplies of potatoes, pigs and flax.[[15]](#cite_note-HW5-1-15)[[16]](#cite_note-HW7-16) In 1835 a [Church Missionary Society](/wiki/Church_Missionary_Society "Church Missionary Society") mission station was established at Tauranga by William Wade. Rev. [Alfred N. Brown](/wiki/Alfred_Nesbit_Brown "Alfred Nesbit Brown") arrived at the CMS mission station in 1838.[[17]](#cite_note-LMR1874-17) John Morgan also visited the mission in 1838.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Street_on_the_waterfront_at_Tauranga%2C_1924._ATLIB_296375.png/250px-Street_on_the_waterfront_at_Tauranga%2C_1924._ATLIB_296375.png)](/wiki/File:Street_on_the_waterfront_at_Tauranga,_1924._ATLIB_296375.png)

View of waterfront in 1924

Europeans trading in [flax](/wiki/Phormium "Phormium") were active in the Bay of Plenty during the 1830s; some were transient, others married local women and settled permanently. The first permanent non-Maori trader was [James Farrow](/wiki/James_Farrow_(trader) "James Farrow (trader)"), who travelled to Tauranga in 1829, obtaining flax fibre for Australian merchants in exchange for [muskets](/wiki/Musket "Musket") and [gunpowder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder"). Farrow acquired a land area of 2,000 square metres (1⁄2 acre) on 10 January 1838 at [Otūmoetai Pā](/wiki/Ot%C5%ABmoetai "Otūmoetai") from the chiefs [Tupaea](/wiki/Hori_Kingi_Tupaea "Hori Kingi Tupaea"), Tangimoana and Te Omanu, the earliest authenticated land purchase in the Bay of Plenty.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In 1840, a [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") mission station was established. [Bishop Pompallier](/wiki/Jean_Baptiste_Pompallier "Jean Baptiste Pompallier") was given land within the palisades of Otūmoetai Pā for a church and a [presbytery](/wiki/Presbytery_(residence) "Presbytery (residence)"). The mission station closed in 1863 due to land wars in the [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato "Waikato") district.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### New Zealand Wars–Tauranga Campaign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: New Zealand Wars–Tauranga Campaign")]

The [Tauranga Campaign](/wiki/Tauranga_Campaign "Tauranga Campaign") took place in and around Tauranga from 21 January to 21 June 1864, during the [New Zealand Wars](/wiki/New_Zealand_Wars "New Zealand Wars"). The [Battle of Gate Pā](/wiki/Tauranga_Campaign#Battle_of_Gate_Pā "Tauranga Campaign") is the best known.

The Battle of Gate Pā was an attack on the well fortified [Pā](/wiki/P%C4%81 "Pā") and its Māori defenders on 29 April 1864 by British forces made up of approximately 300 men of the 43rd Regiment and a naval contingent. The British casualties were 31 dead (including 10 officers), and 80 wounded – the highest loss of life suffered by the British military in the New Zealand Wars. The Māori defenders abandoned the Pā during the night with casualties estimated at 25 dead and an unknown number of wounded.[[21]](#cite_note-cowan42-21)

### Fires

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Fires")]

In November 1916, a large fire broke out on The Strand, destroying the Commercial Hotel and 11 other buildings.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Tauranga_Fire_Hotel.jpg/250px-Tauranga_Fire_Hotel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tauranga_Fire_Hotel.jpg)

Tauranga Hotel on fire surrounded by large crowd

In 1936 another large fire occurred which started in the hotel's staff quarters and drew large crowds.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Modern era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Modern era")]

Under the *Local Government (Tauranga City Council) Order 2003*,[[24]](#cite_note-24) Tauranga became legally a [city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_New_Zealand "List of cities in New Zealand") for a second time, from 1 March 2004.

In August 2011, Tauranga received [Ultra-Fast Broadband](/wiki/Ultra-Fast_Broadband "Ultra-Fast Broadband") as part of the [New Zealand Government](/wiki/New_Zealand_Government "New Zealand Government")'s rollout.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Tauranga%2C_New_Zealand_%28ISS018-E-20020%29.jpg/250px-Tauranga%2C_New_Zealand_%28ISS018-E-20020%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tauranga,_New_Zealand_(ISS018-E-20020).jpg)

Satellite view of Tauranga and surrounding areas

Tauranga is located around a large harbour that extends along the western [Bay of Plenty](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty "Bay of Plenty"), and is protected by [Matakana Island](/wiki/Matakana_Island "Matakana Island") and the extinct volcano of [Mauao](/wiki/Mount_Maunganui_(mountain) "Mount Maunganui (mountain)") (Mount Maunganui). [Ngāumuwahine River](/wiki/Ng%C4%81umuwahine_River "Ngāumuwahine River") is located 19 kilometres southwest of Tauranga.

Tauranga and the [Bay of Plenty](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty "Bay of Plenty") are situated along a faultline and so experience (infrequent) seismic activity. There are a few [volcanoes](/wiki/Volcanoes "Volcanoes") around the area, including both dormant volcanoes such as Mauao, nicknamed "The Mount" by locals, and active volcanoes such as [White Island](/wiki/Whakaari/White_Island "Whakaari/White Island").

Tauranga is roughly the [antipode](/wiki/Antipodes "Antipodes") of [Jaén](/wiki/Ja%C3%A9n,_Spain "Jaén, Spain"), Spain.

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Climate")]

Tauranga has an [oceanic or maritime temperate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") climate (cfb) in the [Köppen Climate Classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"). Though in the [Trewartha Climate Classification](/wiki/Trewartha_climate_classification "Trewartha climate classification") it is [subtropical](/wiki/Subtropical "Subtropical") (cfbl)[[26]](#cite_note-27)

During the summer months the population swells as holidaymakers descend on the city, especially along the popular white coastal surf beaches from [Mount Maunganui](/wiki/Mount_Maunganui "Mount Maunganui") to [Papamoa](/wiki/Papamoa "Papamoa").

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Demographics")]

In 1976 Tauranga was a medium-sized urban area with a population of around 48,000. The completion of a harbour bridge in 1988 brought Tauranga and The Mount closer (they amalgamated in 1989) and re-energised the economies of both parts of the enlarged city.
By 1996 Tauranga's population had grown to 82,092 and by 2006 had reached 103,635.[[31]](#cite_note-32) By 2023, it had reached 152,844.[[32]](#cite_note-Census_2023-33)

In 2008 Tauranga overtook [Dunedin](/wiki/Dunedin "Dunedin") to become the [sixth-largest](/wiki/Ranked_list_of_New_Zealand_main_urban_areas "Ranked list of New Zealand main urban areas") city in New Zealand by urban area, and the [ninth largest](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_New_Zealand "List of cities in New Zealand") city by [Territorial Authority](/wiki/Territorial_Authorities_of_New_Zealand "Territorial Authorities of New Zealand") area. With continuing growth it has now surpassed the Napier-Hastings area to become New Zealand's fifth-largest city.

Tauranga covers 141.91 km2 (54.79 sq mi)[[4]](#cite_note-Area-4) and had an estimated population of 161,000 as of June 2025,[[5]](#cite_note-NZ_population_data_2018-5) with a population density of 1,135 people per km2.

Tauranga had a population of 152,844 in the [2023 New Zealand census](/wiki/2023_New_Zealand_census "2023 New Zealand census"), an increase of 15,714 people (11.5%) since the [2018 census](/wiki/2018_New_Zealand_census "2018 New Zealand census"), and an increase of 37,683 people (32.7%) since the [2013 census](/wiki/2013_New_Zealand_census "2013 New Zealand census"). There were 73,821 males, 78,558 females and 462 people of [other genders](/wiki/Non-binary_gender "Non-binary gender") in 55,929 dwellings.[[34]](#cite_note-35) 2.5% of people identified as [LGBTIQ+](/wiki/LGBTQ "LGBTQ"). The median age was 39.4 years (compared with 38.1 years nationally). There were 29,604 people (19.4%) aged under 15 years, 26,316 (17.2%) aged 15 to 29, 66,786 (43.7%) aged 30 to 64, and 30,138 (19.7%) aged 65 or older.[[32]](#cite_note-Census_2023-33)

People could identify as more than one ethnicity. The results were 78.7% [European](/wiki/European_New_Zealanders "European New Zealanders") ([Pākehā](/wiki/P%C4%81keh%C4%81 "Pākehā")); 19.3% [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people"); 3.6% [Pasifika](/wiki/Pasifika_New_Zealanders "Pasifika New Zealanders"); 10.3% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_New_Zealanders "Asian New Zealanders"); 1.8% Middle Eastern, Latin American and African New Zealanders (MELAA); and 2.1% other, which includes people giving their ethnicity as "New Zealander". English was spoken by 96.2%, Māori language by 4.5%, Samoan by 0.4% and other languages by 12.5%. No language could be spoken by 2.1% (e.g. too young to talk). [New Zealand Sign Language](/wiki/New_Zealand_Sign_Language "New Zealand Sign Language") was known by 0.4%. The percentage of people born overseas was 25.2, compared with 28.8% nationally.

Religious affiliations were 31.3% [Christian](/wiki/Christianity_in_New_Zealand "Christianity in New Zealand"), 1.5% [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism_in_New_Zealand "Hinduism in New Zealand"), 0.4% [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_New_Zealand "Islam in New Zealand"), 1.9% [Māori religious beliefs](/wiki/Religion_of_M%C4%81ori_people "Religion of Māori people"), 0.6% [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism_in_New_Zealand "Buddhism in New Zealand"), 0.4% [New Age](/wiki/New_Age "New Age"), 0.1% [Jewish](/wiki/Judaism_in_New_Zealand "Judaism in New Zealand"), and 3.3% other religions. People who answered that they had [no religion](/wiki/Irreligion_in_New_Zealand "Irreligion in New Zealand") were 53.7%, and 6.9% of people did not answer the census question.

Of those at least 15 years old, 22,431 (18.2%) people had a bachelor's or higher degree, 64,740 (52.5%) had a post-high school certificate or diploma, and 29,052 (23.6%) people exclusively held high school qualifications. The median income was $42,100, compared with $41,500 nationally. 14,001 people (11.4%) earned over $100,000 compared to 12.1% nationally. The employment status of those at least 15 was that 61,218 (49.7%) people were employed full-time, 17,007 (13.8%) were part-time, and 3,252 (2.6%) were unemployed.[[32]](#cite_note-Census_2023-33)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

For elections to the [New Zealand Parliament](/wiki/New_Zealand_Parliament "New Zealand Parliament"), the city of Tauranga is in the [Tauranga](/wiki/Tauranga_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Tauranga (New Zealand electorate)"), [Bay of Plenty](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Bay of Plenty (New Zealand electorate)"), and [Waiariki](/wiki/Waiariki_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Waiariki (New Zealand electorate)") electorates.

Tauranga is located in the administrative area of the [Tauranga City Council](/wiki/Tauranga_City_Council "Tauranga City Council"). The council consists of the [Mayor of Tauranga](/wiki/Mayor_of_Tauranga "Mayor of Tauranga") and nine councillors. The mayor is elected by the city [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large"), while the councillors are elected from nine wards (constituencies), each ward electing a single councillor. Elections are held via [single transferable vote](/wiki/Single_transferable_vote "Single transferable vote").[[43]](#cite_note-44)

The present nine wards were first established for the 2024 local elections. There are eight general wards (Mauao/Mount Maunganui, Arataki, Pāpāmoa, Welcome Bay, Matua-Otūmoetai, Bethlehem, Tauriko and Te Papa) and one [Māori ward](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_wards_and_constituencies "Māori wards and constituencies") (Te Awanui, covering the entire city).[[44]](#cite_note-45)

Council elections are usually held every three years, most recently in [2024](/wiki/2024_Tauranga_local_elections "2024 Tauranga local elections"). The next local election for Tauranga is scheduled for 2028.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

In December 2020, the [Minister of Local Government](/wiki/Minister_of_Local_Government_(New_Zealand) "Minister of Local Government (New Zealand)") [Nanaia Mahuta](/wiki/Nanaia_Mahuta "Nanaia Mahuta") announced that, due to alleged "dysfunction" within the elected council, the council would be replaced by commissioners until the 2022 local elections.[[46]](#cite_note-47)[[47]](#cite_note-48) However, then Tauranga MP [Simon Bridges](/wiki/Simon_Bridges "Simon Bridges") said the appointment of commissioners was unnecessary and a "dramatic and draconian step."[[48]](#cite_note-49) An independent review by law firm [Russell McVeagh](/wiki/Russell_McVeagh "Russell McVeagh") found that Mahuta's decision may have been unlawful.[[49]](#cite_note-50) Her decision to reappoint the [crown commission](/wiki/Tauranga_City_Council#Commission_reappointment_controversy "Tauranga City Council") for a second term in 2022 through to July 2024 was subject to a legal review by [Dentons](/wiki/Dentons "Dentons") Kensington Swan who found her decision was challengeable on the grounds of unlawfulness and unreasonableness.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Economy")]

Much of the countryside surrounding Tauranga is horticultural land, used to grow a wide range of fresh produce for both domestic consumption and export. There are many [kiwifruit](/wiki/Kiwifruit "Kiwifruit") and [avocados](/wiki/Avocados "Avocados") orchards as well as other crops.

The [Port of Tauranga](/wiki/Port_of_Tauranga "Port of Tauranga") is New Zealand's largest export port. It is a regular stop for both container ships and luxury cruise liners.

Tauranga's main shopping malls are Bayfair, in Mount Maunganui and Tauranga Crossing in Tauriko. Most of the city's shopping centres are located in the suburbs. ## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Religion")]

A wide variety of faiths are practised, including [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism"), [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism") and [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"). There are many denominations of Christianity including [Pentecostal](/wiki/Pentecostal "Pentecostal"), [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist"), [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian"), [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic"), [Exclusive Brethren](/wiki/Exclusive_Brethren "Exclusive Brethren"), [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist"), [The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") (LDS Church) and [Jacobite Syrian Christian Church](/wiki/Jacobite_Syrian_Christian_Church "Jacobite Syrian Christian Church").

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Music")]

The National Jazz Festival takes place in Tauranga every Easter.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

### Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Events")]

New Year celebrations at the Mount in [Mount Maunganui](/wiki/Mount_Maunganui "Mount Maunganui") are one of Tauranga's main events, bringing people from all around the country.

In 2014 Tauranga City Council granted permission for an annual Sikh parade to celebrate [Guru Gobind Singh](/wiki/Guru_Gobind_Singh "Guru Gobind Singh")'s birthday. 2500 people took part in 2014, while in 2015, the number increased to 3500.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Abaconda_park-1.jpg/250px-Abaconda_park-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abaconda_park-1.jpg)

McLaren Falls Park, on the outskirts of Tauranga

Tauranga has a large stadium complex in the Mount Manganui suburb, [Baypark Stadium](/wiki/Baypark_Stadium "Baypark Stadium"), rebuilt in 2001 after a similar complex closed in 1995. It hosts [speedway](/wiki/Dirt_track_racing_in_New_Zealand "Dirt track racing in New Zealand") events during summer and [rugby](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football") matches in winter.

Tauranga is also the home of [football (soccer)](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club [Tauranga City United](/wiki/Tauranga_City_United "Tauranga City United") who compete in the [Lotto Sport Italia NRFL Division 2](/wiki/Lotto_Sport_Italia_NRFL_Division_2 "Lotto Sport Italia NRFL Division 2").

Tauranga is the home to two rowing clubs – Tauranga Rowing Club in [Memorial Park](/wiki/Memorial_Park,_Tauranga "Memorial Park, Tauranga") and Bay of Plenty Coast Rowing Club at the picturesque [Wairoa River](/wiki/Wairoa_River_(Bay_of_Plenty) "Wairoa River (Bay of Plenty)"). Both clubs have had successful NZ representation over the years.

Tauranga has an all weather outdoor athletics ground at [Tauranga Domain](/wiki/Tauranga_Domain "Tauranga Domain").

Tauranga also has a [Hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey") Association,[[61]](#cite_note-62) separate from the Regional Bay of Plenty body, which represents the city in domestic tournaments.

## City facilities and attractions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: City facilities and attractions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Mt_Maunganui_%26_Tauranga_2006.jpg/250px-Mt_Maunganui_%26_Tauranga_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mt_Maunganui_%26_Tauranga_2006.jpg)

View over Greater Tauranga, taken from the top of Mauao

Greater Tauranga is a very popular lifestyle and tourism destination. It features many natural attractions and scenery ranging from popular beaches and harbour environments to lush bush-clad mountains with waterfalls and lakes.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Tauranga_strand_%26_fisherman_2017.jpg/250px-Tauranga_strand_%26_fisherman_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tauranga_strand_%26_fisherman_2017.jpg)

View of The Strand, looking across Tauranga Harbour

Cultural attractions include the Tauranga Art Gallery, which opened in October 2007 and showcases local, national and international exhibitions in a range of media. On the 17th Avenue, the "Historic Village on 17th",[[62]](#cite_note-63) recreates a historic setting with original and replica buildings from early Tauranga housing arts and gift shops.

The [Baycourt Community and Arts Centre](/wiki/Baycourt_Community_and_Arts_Centre "Baycourt Community and Arts Centre") is a multi-purpose performing arts and theatre facility located in the central business district.

Aviation interests are well served with the [Classic Flyers Museum](/wiki/Classic_Flyers_Museum "Classic Flyers Museum") and the Gyrate Flying Club where you can experience flying a modern gyroplane; the "motorbike of the sky".[[63]](#cite_note-64)

Tauranga has many parks: one of the largest is [Memorial Park](/wiki/Memorial_Park,_Tauranga "Memorial Park, Tauranga"), and others include Yatton Park, Kulim Park, Fergusson Park and the large Tauranga Domain. The [Te Puna Quarry Park](/w/index.php?title=Te_Puna_Quarry_Park&action=edit&redlink=1 "Te Puna Quarry Park (page does not exist)") has become a regional attraction, known for being converted from a disused quarry into a community park.[[64]](#cite_note-65)

Due to the temperate climate, outdoor activities are very popular, including golf, tramping (hiking), mountain biking and white water rafting. The Bay of Plenty coastline has miles of golden sandy beaches, and watersports are very popular, including swimming, surfing, fishing, diving, kayaking and kitesurfing. Tourists also enjoy dolphin-watching on specially run boat trips.

The coastal suburb [Papamoa](/wiki/Papamoa "Papamoa") and neighbouring [Mount Maunganui](/wiki/Mount_Maunganui "Mount Maunganui") are some of the more affluent areas in Tauranga. The region's beaches attract swimmers, surfers, kayakers and kitesurfers throughout the year.

Tauranga has many outlying islands and reefs that make it a notable tourist destination point for travelling scuba divers and marine enthusiasts.[[65]](#cite_note-66) Extensive marine life diversity is available to scuba divers all year round. Water temperatures range from 12 degrees Celsius in winter to 22–24 degrees Celsius in summer. Tauranga houses two professional dive instructor training centres, training NAUI, PADI and SSI dive leader systems.

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

### Hospitals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Hospitals")]

Tauranga Hospital is a public secondary regional hospital located in Tauranga South, with 360 beds including neonatal, geriatric, surgical, maternity and mental health care.[[66]](#cite_note-67) It provides elective and emergency healthcare across medical, surgical, paediatric, obstetric, gynaecological and psychiatric services. The main tertiary referral centre for Tauranga Hospital is [Waikato Hospital](/wiki/Waikato_Hospital "Waikato Hospital"), located in [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_New_Zealand "Hamilton, New Zealand"). As the site of the Bay of Plenty Clinical School, Tauranga Hospital provides training to medical students from the [University of Auckland](/wiki/University_of_Auckland "University of Auckland"), as well as selective and elective placements for nursing and midwifery students.[[67]](#cite_note-68)

Grace Hospital is Tauranga's only private specialist surgical hospital, located in Oropi. It accommodates 6 operating theatres, 48 inpatient beds, a two-bed HDU, a procedure room for minor surgery and two procedure rooms for endoscopy.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

### Utilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Utilities")]

[Powerco](/wiki/Powerco "Powerco") operates the local distribution network in the city,[[69]](#cite_note-70) with electricity supplied from [Transpower](/wiki/Transpower_New_Zealand_Limited "Transpower New Zealand Limited")'s national grid at three substations: [Greerton](/wiki/Greerton "Greerton"), [Kaitemako](/wiki/Kaitemako "Kaitemako") and [Matapihi](/wiki/Matapihi "Matapihi").

Natural gas arrived in Tauranga in 1982, following the completion of the high-pressure pipeline from the [Maui pipeline](/wiki/Maui_gas_field "Maui gas field") near [Te Awamutu](/wiki/Te_Awamutu "Te Awamutu") to the city, now operated by [First Gas](/wiki/First_Gas "First Gas").[[70]](#cite_note-71) First Gas also operates the gas distribution network within the city.

From 24 October 2024, the [Tauranga City Council](/wiki/Tauranga_City_Council "Tauranga City Council") began [fluoridating](/wiki/Water_fluoridation "Water fluoridation") the city's water supply in response to a directive from the Director-General of Health.[[71]](#cite_note-72)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![East Coast Main Trunk Railway station in Tauranga City](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/00_2023_Tauranga_%28New_Zealand%29_-_Herries_Park_and_Railway_station.jpg/250px-00_2023_Tauranga_%28New_Zealand%29_-_Herries_Park_and_Railway_station.jpg)](/wiki/File:00_2023_Tauranga_(New_Zealand)_-_Herries_Park_and_Railway_station.jpg)

A [KiwiRail train](/wiki/KiwiRail "KiwiRail") on the East Coast Main Trunk Railway which runs through the central city.

[Tauranga City Council](/wiki/Tauranga_City_Council "Tauranga City Council") is currently responsible for approximately 530 km of roads, 700 km of footpaths, cycle ways and access ways.[[72]](#cite_note-73)

Tauranga City Council also has a bit of work under way with their Transportation and Roads strategy. Their aim for the future to change current [travel behaviour](/wiki/Travel_behavior "Travel behavior") from a focus on private cars to more sustainable modes such as buses, cycling and walking.

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Air")]

[Tauranga Airport](/wiki/Tauranga_Airport "Tauranga Airport") is served by [Air New Zealand](/wiki/Air_New_Zealand "Air New Zealand") with flights to Auckland, Wellington and Christchurch. [Sunair](/wiki/Sunair "Sunair") is based in Tauranga, operating a fleet of [light aircraft](/wiki/Light_aircraft "Light aircraft"). Sunair operates from [Tauranga Airport](/wiki/Tauranga_Airport "Tauranga Airport") to [Whangarei](/wiki/Whangarei_Airport "Whangarei Airport"), [Claris](/wiki/Great_Barrier_Aerodrome "Great Barrier Aerodrome"), [Whitianga](/wiki/Whitianga_Aerodrome "Whitianga Aerodrome") and [Mōtītī Island](/wiki/M%C5%8Dt%C4%ABt%C4%AB_Island "Mōtītī Island"). [Barrier Air](/wiki/Barrier_Air "Barrier Air") also operates from Tauranga to Great Barrier Island.

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Rail")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Tauranga_train_bridge.jpg/250px-Tauranga_train_bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tauranga_train_bridge.jpg)

Tauranga railway bridge

Tauranga is located on the [East Coast Main Trunk Railway](/wiki/East_Coast_Main_Trunk_Railway "East Coast Main Trunk Railway").

Tauranga has no passenger rail network, however it is a busy freight rail hub due to distribution from the Port of Tauranga.

### Bus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Bus")]

Main transportation in the city is provided by the BayBus, with twelve routes servicing the city's population. Bay Hopper buses depart the central stops in Tauranga's CBD, Ohauiti, Mount Maunganui and Bayfair every 15 minutes, with the routes to Papamoa and Greerton half-hourly.[[73]](#cite_note-74) [Bee Cards](/wiki/Bee_Card_(New_Zealand) "Bee Card (New Zealand)") were introduced for fares on 27 July 2020.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

The city is also a waypoint for bus travel between cities, with the Bay Hopper, and Intercity having a daily schedule.

### Sea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Sea")]

Tauranga has a passenger ferry service that transports passengers from Tauranga CBD to Mount Maunganui's Salisbury wharf. It is a 2-Way service that costs [$](/wiki/New_Zealand_dollar "New Zealand dollar")15 each way.[[75]](#cite_note-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tauranga&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [List of schools in the Bay of Plenty Region § Tauranga City](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_the_Bay_of_Plenty_Region#Tauranga_City "List of schools in the Bay of Plenty Region")

Tauranga is home to the Bay of Plenty Tertiary Education Partnership, made up of:

* [Toi Ohomai Institute of Technology](/wiki/Toi_Ohomai_Institute_of_Technology "Toi Ohomai Institute of Technology")
* The [University of Waikato](/wiki/University_of_Waikato "University of Waikato")
* [Te Whare Wānanga o Awanuiārangi](/wiki/Te_Whare_W%C4%81nanga_o_Awanui%C4%81rangi "Te Whare Wānanga o Awanuiārangi")[[77]](#cite_note-78)

The organisations currently share two main campuses, but are planning a new central campus. Stage 1 was expected to be open in 2017, catering for 500 but with capacity for 700, which cost $67.3 million.[[78]](#cite_note-79)[[79]](#cite_note-80)

Tauranga's secondary schools are:

* [Tauranga Boys' College](/wiki/Tauranga_Boys%27_College "Tauranga Boys' College"), with about 2100 boys.[[80]](#cite_note-81)
* [Tauranga Girls' College](/wiki/Tauranga_Girls%27_College "Tauranga Girls' College"), with nearly 1500 girls.[[81]](#cite_note-82)
* [Otumoetai College](/wiki/Otumoetai_College "Otumoetai College"), with nearly 1900 students.[[82]](#cite_note-83)
* [Bethlehem College](/wiki/Bethlehem_Campus "Bethlehem Campus"), a state integrated Christian school offering [kindergarten](/wiki/Kindergarten "Kindergarten") and Year 1–13, with around 1500 students.
* [Aquinas College](/wiki/Aquinas_College,_Tauranga "Aquinas College, Tauranga") a state integrated coeducational Catholic school founded in 2003 for Years 7–13, with around 800 students.
* [Mount Maunganui College](/wiki/Mount_Maunganui_College "Mount Maunganui College"), a co-educational secondary school, with over 1500 students.
* [Pāpāmoa College](/wiki/P%C4%81p%C4%81moa_College "Pāpāmoa College"), co-educational secondary school opened in 2011 for years 7–13.
* Te Wharekura o Mauao,[[83]](#cite_note-84) a co-educational wharekura-ā-iwi total immersion Māori secondary school for Years 7–13, founded in 2010, with around 200 students.
* Te Kura Kaupapa Māori o Te Kura Kōkiri, a co-educational [kura kaupapa Māori](/wiki/Kura_kaupapa_M%C4%81ori "Kura kaupapa Māori") total immersion school for Years 1–13, founded in 2000, with around 140 students.

[ACG Tauranga](/wiki/ACG_Tauranga "ACG Tauranga"), the city's first fully [private](/wiki/Private_school#New_Zealand "Private school") school,[[84]](#cite_note-85)[[85]](#cite_note-86) offers school to Year 12.[[86]](#cite_note-87)
